skill.name: authentication-logic
skill.description: Guide to using Better Auth for client and server-side authentication in a Next.js project.
skill.summary: Apply Better Auth client and server patterns for sign-in, sign-out, and session checks.
skill.author: AbdulSamad94

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported shell execution and weak cryptography patterns, but review found Markdown formatting and documentation examples only. No executable command runner, cryptographic implementation, network exfiltration, or prompt injection text was found in SKILL.md.

low_findings.title: Dismissed Static Alert: Markdown Backticks
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged Markdown backticks as Ruby or shell execution. These locations are inline code labels and fenced TypeScript examples, not executable skill logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The marked content is visible Markdown syntax and TypeScript documentation. There is no shell command, command interpolation, or executable script in the skill file.

low_findings.title: Dismissed Static Alert: Weak Cryptography
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography alert points to the description line for Better Auth. The file does not define hashes, ciphers, tokens, or custom cryptographic operations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 3 is plain frontmatter text describing an authentication library. I did not find evidence of a weak cryptographic algorithm or custom crypto implementation.

content.value_statement: Authentication code often fails when client and server checks are inconsistent. This skill gives concise Better Auth patterns for sign-in, sign-out, and protected routes.
actual_capabilities: Identifies the expected Better Auth client module at lib/auth-client.ts.
Identifies the expected Better Auth server module at lib/auth.ts.
Shows email sign-in and Google social sign-in calls through authClient.
Shows sign-out through the Better Auth client.
Shows server-side session checks with auth.api.getSession and request headers.
Documents an AuthBar component that displays an avatar or login button.
limitations: Does not create Better Auth configuration files from scratch.
Does not cover database schema setup, adapters, or migrations.
Does not include password policy, rate limiting, or multi-factor authentication guidance.
Assumes a Next.js project that already uses the listed module paths.

use_cases.title: Add Sign-In Controls
use_cases.description: Use the client examples to add email, Google, and sign-out actions to a React client component.

use_cases.title: Protect Server Workflows
use_cases.description: Use the server session pattern to guard API routes or Server Actions before running protected logic.

use_cases.title: Review Authentication Structure
use_cases.description: Check whether a project uses consistent Better Auth client, server, and AuthBar integration points.

prompt_templates.title: Find Auth Entry Points
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of an existing project
prompt_templates.prompt: Find the Better Auth client and server modules in this project. Explain how they match the authentication-logic skill.

prompt_templates.title: Add Client Sign-In
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the authentication-logic skill to add email sign-in, Google sign-in, and sign-out to the current client component.

prompt_templates.title: Protect a Server Route
prompt_templates.scenario: Add access control to backend code
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the authentication-logic skill to protect this API route with auth.api.getSession and return Unauthorized when no session exists.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Auth Flow Consistency
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the project authentication flow against this skill. Report missing client, server, and AuthBar integration points with file-specific recommendations.

output_examples.input: Review a client component that needs login and logout actions.
output: The response identifies where to import the Better Auth client.
It describes email sign-in, Google sign-in, and sign-out changes.
It notes any missing state handling or error handling that the project should add.

output_examples.input: Protect a server-side handler that currently allows anonymous access.
output: The response explains where to retrieve the current session.
It describes the Unauthorized response for missing sessions.
It confirms that protected logic should run only after the session check.

best_practices: Keep Better Auth client calls inside client components.
Check the session on the server before running protected actions.
Use a shared AuthBar so login state appears consistently across the app.
anti_patterns: Do not trust client-side state as the only access control check.
Do not call server-only auth modules from client components.
Do not expose protected API results before confirming a valid session.

faq.question: What authentication library does this skill cover?
faq.answer: It covers Better Auth usage for client and server authentication patterns.

faq.question: Does this skill configure Better Auth from scratch?
faq.answer: No. It assumes the project already has Better Auth modules in the documented paths.

faq.question: Can it help protect API routes?
faq.answer: Yes. It shows the server-side session check pattern for routes and Server Actions.

faq.question: Does it include social login guidance?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes a Google social sign-in example with an onboarding callback.

faq.question: Does it handle user interface state?
faq.answer: It references an AuthBar component that shows a user avatar or login button.

faq.question: Is this skill safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes. The reviewed file is documentation-only, and static alerts were dismissed as false positives.
